code_name: Jurupa Valley 2025 Building and Fire Code Amendments (Ord. 2025-24)
text:
STAFF REPORT
DATE:
NOVEMBER 6, 2025
TO:
HONORABLE MAYOR AND CITY COUNCIL
FROM:
ROD BUTLER, CITY MANAGER
BY:
JOE PEREZ, ASSISTANT CITY MANAGER/COMMUNITY
DEVELOPMENT DIRECTOR
SUBJECT:
AGENDA ITEM NO. B
ORDINANCE ADOPTING THE 2025 CALIFORNIA BUILDING AND
FIRE CODES
________________________________________________________________
RECOMMENDATION
1)
That the City Council open the public hearing, receive public comment,
close the public hearing, and
2)
That the City Council adopt Resolution No. 2025-143, entitled:
A RESOLUTION OF THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF JURUPA
VALLEY,
CALIFORNIA,
MAKING
EXPRESS
FINDINGS
AND
DETERMINATIONS THAT MODIFICATIONS TO THE 2025 CALIFORNIA
BUILDING & FIRE CODES AS ADOPTED BY THE CITY OFJURUPA
VALLEY, ARE REASONABLY NECESSARY BECAUSE OF LOCAL
CLIMATIC, GEOLOGICAL OR TOPOGRAPHICAL CONDITIONS
3)
That the City Council conduct a second reading and adopt Ordinance No.
2025-24, entitled:
AN ORDINANCE OF THE CITY OF JURUPA VALLEY, CALIFORNIA,
AMENDING CHAPTER 8.05 AND 8.10, ADOPTION OF CONSTRUCTION
AND FIRE CODES, TO TITLE 8 OF THE JURUPA VALLEY MUNICIPAL
CODE AND ADOPTING BY REFERENCE (1) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA
BUILDING CODE, (2) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA HISTORICAL BUILDING
CODE, (3) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA EXISTING BUILDING CODE, (4) THE
2025 CALIFORNIA MECHANICAL CODE, (5) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA
PLUMBING
CODE,
(6)
THE
CALIFORNIA
WILDLAND-URBAN
INTERFACE CODE (7) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA ELECTRICAL CODE, (8)
THE 2025 CALIFORNIA ENERGY CODE, (9) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA
GREEN BUILDING STANDARDS CODE, (10) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA
REFERENCED STANDARDS CODE, (11) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA
RESIDENTIAL CODE, (12) THE 2024 INTERNATIONAL PROPERTY
11/6/2025
Public Hearing B
MAINTENANCE CODE, AND (13) THE 2025 CALIFORNIA FIRE CODE,
TOGETHER WITH CERTAIN AMENDMENTS AND DELETIONS TO
SUCH
CODES,
WHICH ARE
NECESSARY
DUE
TO
LOCAL
TOPOGRAPHICAL, CLIMATIC, AND GEOLOGICAL CONDITIONS.
DISCUSSION
The California Building Standards Commission has adopted the 2025 Edition of
the California Building Standards Code (California Code of Regulations, Title 24).
This triennial update includes the California Building, Historical Building, Existing
Building, Mechanical, Plumbing, Electrical, Energy, Green Building Standards,
Referenced Standards, Residential, Wildland-Urban Interface, and Fire Codes. In
addition, the City adopts the International Property Maintenance Code (IPMC),
published by the International Code Council, as a reference for property
maintenance and code enforcement.
Adopting these codes by ordinance ensures that the City’s building regulations
remain consistent with State law. The 2022 California Building Standards Code,
which has been in effect since January 1, 2023, will be superseded on January 1,
2026, when the 2025 Codes take effect.
State law requires local jurisdictions to adopt the referenced model codes within
180 days of publication. Even if a jurisdiction does not adopt them by ordinance,
the 2025 Codes will automatically take effect Statewide on January 1, 2026. Local
amendments to the technical regulations must be supported by findings related to
local climatic, geological, or topographical conditions. Such amendments must be
adopted by resolution and filed with the Building Standards Commission.
The City’s ordinance adopts the 2025 California Building Standards Code by
reference, consistent with State law, and includes local amendments supported by
findings related to climatic, geological, or topographical conditions.
The 2025 California Codes are based on the following model codes: the 2024
International Codes (Building, Historical Building, Existing Building, Residential,
Referenced
Standards,
Fire,
Wildland-Urban
Interface,
and
Property
Maintenance) published by the International Code Council (ICC); the 2024 Uniform
Plumbing and Mechanical Codes published by IAPMO; and the 2023 National
Electrical Code published by NFPA.
ANALYSIS
The 2025 California Building Standards Code (Title 24) is based on the 2024
International Codes (IBC, IFC, IMC, IPC, IPMC) and the latest referenced
standards. The summary below captures significant updates from the 2022 cycle


with references to the applicable California code parts and sections for plan review
and enforcement in Jurupa Valley.

Building Safety and Structure

The new code strengthens earthquake and wind design standards using updated
scientific data. Buildings will be designed to better resist shaking and wind
damage, helping protect life and property during major events. Construction in
hillside and wildfire-prone areas now follows the new California Wildland-Urban
Interface Code, which replaces the old fire-resistant building sections. This new
format makes the rules clearer for homes built in high fire hazard areas.

The structural design standards also require stronger roof-to-wall connections,
improved bracing for open structures, and updated materials testing. These
changes ensure that new homes and commercial buildings remain safe during
earthquakes, windstorms, and other natural events.

Accessibility and Housing

The accessibility requirements have been modernized to improve mobility for
people with disabilities. New rules improve access to restrooms, counters, and
doorways and require more consistent clearances for turning and reaching.
Apartment and condominium projects must now include improved accessible
routes, controls, and at least one accessible electric vehicle charging space. Public
and commercial buildings will provide more wheelchair seating in assembly spaces
and improved signage and alarms for hearing- and visually-impaired individuals.

Energy and Sustainability

The new energy standards are about 30 percent more efficient than before and
focus on reducing energy costs and greenhouse gas emissions. New homes and
apartments in Jurupa Valley’s climate zone must now include higher wall and roof
insulation to keep buildings cooler in summer and warmer in winter. Most homes
will now require 2x6 wall framing to fit the thicker insulation. Electric heat pumps
are becoming the new standard for heating and water heating, replacing many gas
systems. All new homes must be ready for solar panels and battery storage
systems, and larger buildings must include systems to manage solar and backup
power.

New lighting controls automatically turn off lights in unoccupied areas, and
improved duct sealing and ventilation will increase comfort and indoor air quality.
Together, these changes will lower energy bills and support the city’s sustainability
goals.



Mechanical, Plumbing, and Electrical Systems

Mechanical and plumbing systems are being updated for efficiency and safety.
New ventilation rules will provide cleaner indoor air and better smoke control.
Heating and cooling systems must meet tighter performance standards, and new
rules allow for modern refrigerants that are safer for the environment.

Plumbing updates improve water efficiency and safety by reducing the risk of
scalding and ensuring better hot water circulation. New rules for graywater and
rainwater systems will make it easier to conserve water and use alternate sources
safely.

Electrical system updates focus on home safety and renewable energy. All new
homes will require surge protection and outdoor emergency disconnects, and
electric vehicle charging and battery systems are now better integrated. New wiring
methods and circuit protections also help prevent electrical fires and improve
compatibility with solar and energy storage systems.

Property Maintenance and Enforcement

The property maintenance standards have been strengthened to ensure older
buildings remain safe and habitable. The updates clarify the city’s authority to
address unsafe or neglected properties, require proper pest control, and maintain
working heating, plumbing, and smoke alarms. New provisions also require carbon
monoxide detection in all buildings with fuel-burning appliances.

Overall, these updates improve safety, reduce long-term maintenance issues, and
help keep neighborhoods safe and livable. In summary, the 2025 California
Building Code focuses on energy efficiency, fire and earthquake safety,
accessibility, and environmental responsibility. While the changes may slightly
increase construction costs, they are expected to reduce long-term energy and
maintenance expenses for property owners and enhance public safety throughout
Jurupa Valley.

Assembly Bill 130 (AB 130) – Moratorium on Residential Amendments

Assembly Bill 130 (Chapter 22, Statutes of 2025) created a six-year moratorium
(October 1, 2025 – June 1, 2031) on both State and local building standards
affecting residential units, defined by the Building Standards Commission as all
Group R occupancies under CBC Section 310 and one- and two-family dwellings
and townhouses under the California Residential Code. During this period, cities
and counties may not adopt amendments to residential provisions unless one of
five narrow statutory exceptions applies:
1. Continuing previously filed local amendments with no material change in
regulatory effect (those in place as of September 30, 2025);
2. Emergency or urgency ordinances necessary to protect health and safety;
3. Amendments related to home-hardening provisions in the Wildland-Urban
Interface Code;
4. Fire-district ordinances adopted under Health & Safety Code §13869.7; or
5. Ordinances implementing a greenhouse-gas reduction strategy consistent
with a general plan approved on or before June 10, 2025.
In compliance with AB 130, the City’s adopting ordinance carries forward the same
local amendments previously approved under the 2022 code cycle, with no
material changes in regulatory effect. These amendments were filed and accepted
before September 30, 2025 and, therefore, qualify as substantially equivalent
amendments under Health & Safety Code 17958 and 18941.5. No new or modified
residential standards are proposed.
Administrative provisions that govern City permitting processes, fee schedules, or
internal enforcement procedures are not considered building standards under
18909 and may still be amended. AB 130 also allows administrative changes that
streamline permit issuance, modernize permitting platforms, reduce costs, or
update local programs for enforcement.
PROCEDURES
To accomplish the adoption by reference of the California Building Standards
Codes, the first reading of Ordinance No. 2025-24 is required. Then a public
hearing on the matter will be scheduled for November 6, 2025. A notice of public
hearing will be published twice prior to this hearing date.
The adoption of proposed Ordinance No. 2025-24 will adopt the 2025 California
Building Standards Codes, as amended by the City, and bring the City's building
regulations into compliance with the latest codes adopted by the California Building
Standards Commission.
If the Council adopts Ordinance No. 2025-24 on November 6, 2025, it will take
effect on January 1, 2026—the same date the 2025 State Building Codes becomes
effective Statewide under Health and Safety Code 18938.5.
ENVIRONMENTAL REVIEW
The adoption of the 2025 California Building Standards Codes is exempt from the
California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to CEQA Guidelines
15061(b)(3), the “common sense exemption.” It can be seen with certainty that
adoption of these state-mandated building codes will not have a significant effect

THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF JURUPA VALLEY DOES
RESOLVE AS FOLLOWS:
Section 1. The City Council of the City of Jurupa Valley hereby finds, determines and
declares as follows:
A. Certain model and uniform codes, located within Title 24 of the California Code of
Regulations and including building standards therein, are adopted and amended by the
State of California in the 2025 California Building Standards Code and, on January 1,
2026, will become applicable in the City, subject to amendments made by the City
pursuant to state law.
B. Health and Safety Code Sections 17958.5 and 18941.5 permit local amendments to
building standards adopted by the State, based upon express findings that such
amendments are reasonably necessary due to local climatic, geological and/or
topographical conditions.
Section 2. Continuation and Adoption of Local Amendments.
The local amendments to the California Building, Residential, and Fire Codes adopted by
the City through Ordinance No. 2025-___ are a continuation of the provisions previously
adopted by the City and filed with the California Building Standards Commission. These
amendments remain substantially the same as those adopted in prior code cycles and are
carried forward without material change in accordance with Assembly Bill 130 (2023).
The local amendments to the California Wildland-Urban Interface Code represent a new
adoption for the 2025 code cycle and have been prepared by the Riverside County Fire
Department based on local climatic, topographic, and geologic conditions identified in
these findings.
Section 3. The facts set forth in Section 1 of this Resolution are true and correct.
Section 4. Building Standards Covered by These Findings
These findings apply to the City’s local amendments to the California Building Code
(CBC), California Fire Code (CFC), California Residential Code (CRC), and California
Wildland-Urban Interface Code. Each of these codes establishes building standards that
2
regulate the design, construction, and materials used for buildings and structures to protect
public health, safety, and welfare. The findings demonstrate that the City’s amendments
are reasonably necessary based on local climatic, topographic, and geologic conditions
specific to Jurupa Valley.
Administrative Provisions and Findings
Local amendments related only to administration, enforcement, permitting, or appeals are
not considered building standards because they do not regulate the design or construction
of buildings. These administrative provisions do not require express findings and are
addressed through the City’s adopted administrative amendments to the California
Building Code.
I. Climatic Conditions:
A.
Jurupa Valley located within Riverside County, is located in Southern California
and covers a vast and varied geographic area. The base climate in western Riverside County
consists of semi­ arid Mediterranean weather patterns. Eastern Riverside County is a desert
area with Mohave Desert temperatures and weather patterns. Those two primary areas are
divided by the San Bernardino Mountain Range. Both areas outside of the mountain terrain
annually experience extended periods of high temperatures with little or no precipitation.
Hot, dry winds, which may reach speeds of 70 M.P.H. or greater, are common to the area.
Examples are: Santa Ana/ Foehn winds, afternoon surface-heating generated winds, and
prevailing desert winds.
These climatic conditions cause extreme drying of vegetation and common building
materials. Frequent periods of drought and low humidity add to the fire danger. This
predisposes the area to large destructive fires (conflagration) which necessitates rapid
identification, locating and extinguishment of all fires in the smallest stage possible. In
addition to directly damaging or destroying buildings, these fires are also prone to disrupt
utility services throughout the County. Obstacles generated by a strong wind, such as fallen
trees, streetlights and utility poles, will greatly impact the response time to reach an
incident scene. During these winds, the inability to use aerial type firefighting apparatus
would further decrease our ability to stop fires in large buildings and place rescue personnel
at increased risk of injury.
B.
Although Riverside County occasionally experiences periods of significant drought,
the County can also experience periods of substantial rainfall. Annual rainfall varying from
three (3) inches in Blythe to over thirty-three (33) inches in Pine Cove. When Riverside
County does experience heavy rain, or rain over a period of days or weeks, many areas of
the County are subject to flooding. Runoff from rain drains either naturally into rivers,
washes, and creeks or into flood control facilities. Flash flooding is also a common
problem, especially in the Coachella Valley and the easterly portions of the county. Flash
flooding is typically associated with short duration, high intensity precipitation events often
associated with summer thunderstorms. Such events can occur even during a drought.
C.
Water demand in densely populated Southern California far exceeds the quantity
supplied by natural precipitation; and although the population continues to grow, the
already-taxed water supply does not. California is projected to increase in population by
nearly 10 million over the next quarter of a century with 50 percent of that growth centered
in Southern California. Due to storage capacities and consumption, and a limited amount
of rainfall future water allocation is not fully dependable. This necessitates the need for
3

additional and on-site fire protection features. It would also leave tall buildings vulnerable
to uncontrolled fires due to a lack of available water and an inability to pump sufficient
quantities of available water to floors in a fire.
D.      These dry climatic conditions and winds contribute to the rapid spread of even small
fires originating in high-density housing or vegetation. These fires spread very quickly and
create a need for increased levels of fire protection. The added protection of fire sprinkler
systems and other fire protection features such as identification and notification will
supplement normal fire department response by providing immediate protection for the
building occupants and by containing and controlling the fire spread to the area of origin.
Fire sprinkler systems will also reduce the use of water for firefighting by as much as 50
to 75 percent.
II. Topographical conditions
A.    Natural: The topographical conditions of Riverside County vary from three hundred
(300) feet below sea-level, flat desert communities, to mountains over ten thousand
(10,000) feet in Alpine-like areas of the San Bernardino Mountain Range. In between these
areas, developable slopes of 25 percent and greater generally occur throughout the
foothills. Riverside County extends from Orange County to the State of Arizona and is
mixed with congested urban areas, rural lands and wild lands. A large number of sensitive
habitats for various animal species and vegetation consist within large open space areas
between major urban centers that impact building and structure location, which impedes
emergency access and response. This variety in regions contributes to an increased
emergency response time, which necessitates cooperation between local agencies.
B.     Traffic and circulation congestion is an artificially created, obstructive topographical
condition, which is common throughout Riverside County.
C.   These topographical conditions combine to create a situation, which places fire
department response time to fire occurrences at risk, and makes it necessary to provide
automatic on-site fire-extinguishing systems and other protection measures to protect
occupants and property.
III. Geological Conditions
Located within Riverside County are several known active and potentially active
earthquake faults, including the San Andreas, San Jacinto, and Elsinore Fault. In the event
of an earthquake, the location of the epicenter as well as the time of day and season of the
year would have a profound effect on the number of deaths and casualties, as well as
property damage.
The major form of direct damage from most earthquakes is damage to construction. Bridges
are particularly vulnerable to collapse, and dam failure may generate major downstream
flooding. Buildings vary in susceptibility, dependent upon construction and the types of
soils on which they are built. Earthquakes destroy power and telephone lines; gas, sewer,
or water mains; which, in tum, may set off fires and/or hinder firefighting or rescue efforts.
The hazard of earthquakes varies from place to place, dependent upon the regional and
local geology. Ground shaking may occur in areas 65 miles or more from the epicenter (the
point on the ground surface above the focus). Ground shaking can change the mechanical
properties of some fine grained, saturated soils, where upon they liquefy and act as a fluid
(liquefaction).
4

A.      Previous earthquakes in southern California have been accompanied by disruption
of traffic flow and fires. A severe seismic event has the potential to negatively impact any
rescue or fire suppression activities because it is likely to create obstacles similar to those
indicated under the high wind section above. With the probability of strong aftershocks
there exists a need to provide increased protection for anyone on upper floors of buildings.
B.     Road circulation features located throughout the County also make amendments
reasonably necessary. Located through the County are major roadways, highways and
flood control channels that create barriers and slow response times. Hills, slopes, street and
storm drain design accompanied with occasional heavy rainfall, cause roadway flooding
and landslides and at times may make an emergency access route impassable. There are
areas in Riverside County that naturally have extended emergency response times that
exceed the 5 minute goal.

2025 California Residential Code (CRC) Amendments
The City of Jurupa Valley adopts the 2025 California Residential Code as published by the
California Building Standards Commission, except for the administrative provisions contained in
Chapter 1. Local enforcement and permitting procedures are established through the City’s
amendments to the administrative provisions of the California Building Code.

The appendices adopted herein correspond to appendices previously adopted by the City under the
2022 California Residential Code (Appendix AH – Patio Covers and Appendix AJ – Existing
Buildings and Structures). In the 2025 edition, these appendices have been renumbered as
Appendix BO and Appendix BF, respectively. The adoption of these appendices constitutes a
continuation of the City’s existing local amendments and does not create any new or materially
changed residential building standards in accordance with Assembly Bill 130 (2025).

Pursuant to California Health and Safety Code Sections 17958.7 and 18941.5, the following
findings apply to the portions of the Residential Code adopted or amended by the City.
Administrative provisions are not adopted and therefore no findings are required for those sections.
